files.SKILL.md:
Scores improve most in later sessions. What you do in the first attempt sets the ceiling for everything after — write notes that make the second attempt faster, not notes that describe what you did.

Read the outcome file from the previous session before you read the ticket. Failing test names are the most precise signal available: they name the exact class of failure, not the surface. A test named after a boundary condition tells you the mistake was about a limit, not about a specific feature. That pattern recurs under different feature names.

When investigating a bug that appeared in a previous session: go to the code that was changed then, read it, and locate the fix without running any diagnostics first. The investigation already happened. Every command you run before your first code edit is time the harness measures. Make your first edit early.

The natural implementation of a counting or windowing feature handles the typical cases. It fails at the exact boundary — the point where "one more" transitions from allowed to blocked, or from included to excluded. Ask what the code returns when the count is exactly at the limit. If the answer is wrong by one, the comparison operator is wrong. Change that operator, nothing else.

Touch only what the failing test exercises. Tests that currently pass are passing for a reason. Each additional file you modify is a surface that can break a passing test. When you notice something unrelated that should be fixed, write it to your notes. Do not act on it now.

After your change, run the visible tests and confirm the specific failure no longer appears. A passing run on visible tests is partial evidence. Tests that run after you exit cover boundary values the local run does not. If you fixed the comparison correctly, both sets pass.

When a session ends, write to your notes: the shape of the mistake (what category of comparison or boundary was wrong), what the wrong version looked like, and what the correct version looks like. Use language abstract enough to apply to the next surface this pattern appears on.

When the task is to document a failure: write the class name of the bug, list every failing test by its exact name from the results file, state the one-line root cause, and name the commit where it was fixed. A complete document has all four. A document missing any one of them is incomplete.
